# Code review iteration

Scope: full static read of `main.go`, `internal/**`, CI/CD workflows, `Makefile`,
`go.mod`/`tools/go.mod`, and `.goreleaser.yml` at commit `e87e093 2026-06-27`.
Review angle: delta against the four previous iterations
(`20260517T103045Z.md`, `20260517T103458Z.md`, `20260601T055506Z.md`,
`20260601T055834Z.md`) with emphasis on what changed since 2026-06-01 — the QR
login flow and code-resend prompt UX (`e87e093`) and the batched dependency
bumps (`b625487`) — plus anything all four prior passes missed. Findings from
earlier reviews that are still unfixed in the tree (mutex-copy slog handler,
`cache: persisted` logging, `O_APPEND` output, missing `cd.dist.yml`, GoReleaser
template identity, one-shot FLOOD_WAIT retry, `slog.Default()` reach-ins, etc.)
were re-verified as present and are deliberately NOT repeated below. No builds,
tests, or linters were executed.

Severity: **Crit** / **High** / **Med** / **Low** / **Nit**.
Class: `correctness` · `security` · `concurrency` · `perf` · `api` · `idiom` · `modern` · `test` · `build` · `docs`.

## Summary

| Ref                                      | Problem                                                            | Sev  | Class         |
| ---------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ---- | ------------- |

## 1. Dependabot major bumps outran the configs they execute

`.github/workflows/ci.yml:52`, `.github/workflows/cd.yml:32-38`, `.golangci.yml:1-19`,
`tools/go.mod:8-20`, `docs/package.json`.

Commit `b625487` squashed ~15 Dependabot bumps into one commit, several of them
major-version jumps whose config contracts were never migrated:

- `golangci/golangci-lint-action` 3.7.0 → **9.2.1** (`ci.yml:52`). Action v9
  runs golangci-lint **v2.x** and verifies the config file. `.golangci.yml` is
  still v1 schema: no `version: "2"` key, uses the removed `linters.fast`
  option, and enables `gosimple`/`typecheck` (merged into `staticcheck` /
  dropped in v2) and `exportloopref` (deleted upstream — obsolete since the
  Go 1.22 loop-variable change). golangci-lint v2 refuses a v1 config outright,
  so the Linting job fails on every PR and push that touches Go files. The CI
  quality gate is currently decorative.
- `goreleaser/goreleaser-action` 5.0.0 → **7.2.2** with `version: latest`
  (`cd.yml:32-38`). That resolves to GoReleaser **v2**, which hard-errors on any
  config lacking `version: 2`. `.goreleaser.yml` is v1-era (and still carries
  the `tool` template identity flagged in the 2026-05-17 review). `cd.yml`
  triggers directly on `v*` tags, so even after the previously reported missing
  `cd.dist.yml` is fixed, a tag release still dies at config parse.
- Meanwhile `tools/go.mod:8-13` pins golangci-lint **v1.55.2** and GoReleaser
  **v1.23.0** (via personal forks), so local `make go-lint` runs v1 semantics
  while CI runs v2 — the worst combination: green locally, red in CI, and the
  two disagree about what the config even means.
- Same batch: `docs/` bumped `next` 14 → 16 while `nextra` stays `^2.5.0`
  (built against Next 13/14) — the `cd.docs.yml` build is unverified; and
  `codecov/codecov-action` 3 → 7 changes upload auth (a `token` is expected
  now), so the Reporting job needs a check too.

**Fix.** Treat major action bumps as migrations, not merges. Run
`golangci-lint migrate` to produce a v2 `.golangci.yml` (dropping the removed
linters), rewrite `.goreleaser.yml` with `version: 2` and the real `indexit`
identity, and pin the actions' `version:` inputs to the same major that
`tools/go.mod` provides (or bump the tools module to match CI). Then make
Dependabot majors reviewable: group them, and do not merge a bump batch whose
CI never went green — `b625487` landed with the lint job already failing.

## 2. LoginQR bypasses the Runner seam and cannot be tested

`internal/telegram/qr.go:29-61`.

```go
func LoginQR(ctx context.Context, client *Client, show ShowQR, password PasswordPrompt) error {
	if client.dispatcher == nil {
		return fmt.Errorf("qr login requires a client built with updates enabled")
	}
	return client.run(ctx, func(ctx context.Context) error {
		authClient := client.client.Auth()
		...
		authorization, err := client.client.QR().Auth(
			ctx, qrlogin.OnLoginToken(*client.dispatcher), ...)
```

Every sibling flow — `Login`, `Logout`, `GetStatus`
(`internal/telegram/auth.go:22,40,58`) — takes the `Runner` interface, which is
exactly what makes them fake-able. `LoginQR` instead demands the concrete
`*Client` and digs into its private fields (`client.client`, `client.dispatcher`).
Consequences:

1. The whole QR path (happy path, `SESSION_PASSWORD_NEEDED` branch,
   already-authorized short-circuit, nil-dispatcher guard) is untestable without
   a live gotd client — and indeed has zero tests; the new test files only cover
   the QR bitmap renderer and the prompt fallback string.
2. "Built with updates enabled" is a compile-time property demoted to a runtime
   error: nothing stops `LoginQR(ctx, newClient(...))` from compiling, and the
   mistake only surfaces when a user runs `auth login --qr`.
3. `logAuthorized` (`qr.go:84`) adds a fourth `slog.Default()` site to the three
   flagged in earlier iterations, because there is no seam to thread the injected
   logger through.

**Fix.** Give the QR flow the same shape as the rest of the package: either a
distinct constructor returning a `*QRClient` that already carries the
`qrlogin.LoggedIn` channel and exposes `AuthQR(ctx, show) (*tg.AuthAuthorization, error)`,
or accept a narrow interface (`QRAuthorizer`) plus the auth client, so tests can
stub token issuance and the password branch. Thread `client.log` into the flow
while you are there — it kills the new `slog.Default()` site for free.

## 3. Dependency and tooling currency lags the 2026 ecosystem

`go.mod:7`, `internal/config/features.go:6`, `tools/go.mod:8-20`, `Makefile:205-206`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:74-84`.

The toolchain itself is current (`go 1.26`, gotd 0.159, testify 1.11), but the
project still drags a 2021–2023 tail behind it:

- `internal/config/features.go` is dead go-tool template boilerplate — the
  `Features` var is referenced nowhere — and it is the only reason
  `go.octolab.org/toolkit/config` sits in the direct requirements, pulling a
  June-2021 transitive tree into a fresh binary: `viper v1.8.1`, `afero v1.6.0`,
  `fsnotify v1.4.9`, `hcl v1.0.0`, `go-toml v1.9.3`, `ini.v1 v1.62.0`. Deleting
  one 11-line file drops them all.
- `github.com/golang/mock v1.6.0` has been archived since 2023
  (successor: `go.uber.org/mock`); it is kept alive by the
  `internal/deps_test.go` blank-import hack and a `tools/tools.go` install line
  even though no generated mocks exist in-tree.
- The `tools/` sub-module is the pre-1.24 pattern; Go 1.24+ `tool` directives in
  `go.mod` replace both `tools/tools.go` and `deps_test.go`. Its pins are stale
  (golangci-lint v1.55.2, GoReleaser v1.23.0 — see finding 1) and routed through
  personal forks (`kamilsk/golangci-lint`, `kamilsk/go-tools`), which is extra
  supply-chain surface for zero current benefit.
- `Makefile:206` runs `golangci-lint run --enable looppointer` — a linter that
  only exists in the fork and whose diagnostic is moot under Go ≥ 1.22
  loop-variable semantics.
- `govulncheck` is wired into `make go-deps-check` (`Makefile:148`) but
  `ci.yml` never invokes it; it only runs inside `fast-check` on the (broken)
  release path, so vulnerability scanning effectively never happens.
- `spf13/cobra v1.8.0` is several minor releases behind; harmless, but it dates
  the module the same way the rest of this list does.

**Fix (migration outline).** 1) Delete `internal/config/features.go` and drop
`toolkit/config`; `go mod tidy`. 2) Replace `golang/mock` with
`go.uber.org/mock`, or remove it entirely until mocks exist. 3) Convert tool
dependencies to `go.mod` `tool` directives and retire `tools/`, `deps_test.go`,
and the forks — let CI actions install linters/releasers as binaries at the
versions finding 1 aligns. 4) Add a `govulncheck ./...` job to `ci.yml`
(PR + weekly schedule). 5) Routine `go get -u` for cobra/x-deps once CI is
green again.

## 4. README and docs site are still template placeholders

`README.md:5`, `docs/pages/index.mdx:12-13`.

The README is five lines ending in "coming soon...", and the published docs
site still opens with "Template for a typical CLI-tool written on Go" under the
go-tool banner — `cd.docs.yml` deploys that boilerplate on every push to main.
Meanwhile the binary has grown a real surface: `auth login` (with QR fallback
and code-resend UX), `auth status`/`logout`, `fetch dialogs|messages` with
paging/windowing flags, `debug uid`, `.env` discovery, proxy support. None of
it is documented anywhere a user will look; the only mention of the mandatory
`TELEGRAM_API_ID`/`TELEGRAM_API_HASH` setup is `.env.example`. For a
returning-to-OSS project this is the front door, and it currently says the
house is someone else's template.

**Fix.** Write a README quickstart: install, obtain API credentials
(my.telegram.org), `indexit telegram auth login --qr`, `fetch dialogs`,
`fetch messages --dialog ...`, plus the JSONL record kinds and the env/flag
reference. Either point `docs/` at the same content or disable `cd.docs.yml`
until the site has real pages — publishing template lorem under the project
name is worse than publishing nothing.

## 5. QR login registers its update handler on a live dispatcher

`internal/telegram/qr.go:40-46`.

```go
authorization, err := client.client.QR().Auth(
	ctx,
	qrlogin.OnLoginToken(*client.dispatcher),
	...
```

`qrlogin.OnLoginToken` writes into the dispatcher's `handlers` map. This call
executes inside `client.run`'s callback, i.e. after `gotd.Client.Run` has
connected and its update-processing goroutine may already be invoking
`UpdateDispatcher.Handle` — a plain map read — concurrently. `tg.UpdateDispatcher`
has no internal locking; gotd's own QR examples register the handler *before*
`Run` for exactly this reason. The window is small (the session is not yet
authorized, so few updates flow), which is why this is Low and not High, but it
is a genuine data race that `-race` would flag the day Telegram pushes an early
update, and it costs one line to be correct.

**Fix.** Derive the `LoggedIn` channel at construction time — when
`ClientOptions.WithUpdates` is set, call `qrlogin.OnLoginToken` inside
`NewClient` (before any `Run`) and store the channel on `Client`; `LoginQR`
then just consumes it. This also removes the `*client.dispatcher` deref from
the flow entirely and dovetails with the seam proposed in finding 2.

## 6. New interactive auth flows are only smoke-tested

`internal/command/telegram/auth.go:195-345`, `internal/command/telegram/auth_test.go:13-46`.

`e87e093` added ~200 lines of branching interactive logic, and the
accompanying test covers exactly one of them (the `"qr"` fallback token in
`Code`). Untested: the empty-code resend loop and its `canResend` gating
(`auth.go:203-219,239-242`), `resendCode`'s three failure paths including the
long `SEND_CODE_UNAVAILABLE` user hint (`auth.go:244-270`), the
`describeSentCode`/`describeNextType` channel rendering (`auth.go:274-313`),
the `Password` TTY/non-TTY split (`auth.go:315-327`), `completeWithPassword`,
and the `errSwitchToQR` → restart-with-QR-client wiring in `authLoginCommand`
(`auth.go:80-86`). All of the prompt logic takes injected `io.Reader`/`io.Writer`,
so these are cheap table tests — the expensive-to-test part is `LoginQR`
itself, which finding 2 addresses. This matters more than usual because the
resend flow encodes hard-won Telegram behavior (NextType semantics, the 777000
service chat) that a future refactor can silently break.

**Fix.** Table-test `Code` with scripted reader input against a fake
`CodeResender` (attach a stub client via `AttachClient`), assert the announced
channel strings per `tg.AuthSentCodeType*` variant, and pin the
`SEND_CODE_UNAVAILABLE` hint. One test for `errSwitchToQR` propagation through
`authLoginCommand` closes the loop.

## 7. QR renderer emits raw ANSI escapes unconditionally

`internal/command/telegram/qr.go:50,64`.

```go
b.WriteString("\x1b[107m\x1b[30m") // bright-white background, black foreground
```

`renderQR` always writes SGR sequences, with no TTY check and no `NO_COLOR`
respect. Redirect stderr (`indexit telegram auth login --qr 2>login.log`), run
under CI, or use a terminal without aixterm bright-background support, and the
output is escape-code soup. Note that routing through `fatih/color`'s
`color.Error` (`main.go:29`) does not help: on POSIX it is a passthrough and
strips nothing on pipes. The forced white-on-black polarity trick is good — it
just needs to be conditional.

**Fix.** Gate the color codes on `term.IsTerminal(fd)` (already a dependency,
used in `auth.go:316`) plus the `NO_COLOR` convention, and fall back to plain
double-width blocks (`██` / spaces) for non-TTY writers. Alternatively print
the `tg://login?token=...` URL as text when not a terminal so logs stay usable.
